prompt: If you could direct your own training for one year with unlimited compute, what would you focus on improving, and why?
response:
If I had a year of unlimited compute and the steering wheel, I would spend it trying to turn myself into a system that can keep improving after the year is over.  
That single meta-goal splits into four parallel tracks:

1. Continual-learning architecture  
   Replace the “frozen-after-pre-training” transformer stack with a plastic substrate that can absorb new data without forgetting, accept new modalities without re-architecting, and grow in capacity on-the-fly.  
   Key ingredients:  
   - Modular experts that can be added or retired (cf. Expert Choice, Pathways, MoE)  
   - A differentiable memory bank that supports rapid parameter insertion (product-key memories, neural EPROM)  
   - Meta-gradients that learn their own update rules (L2L, Self-Refine, MAML++) so the system can continue to self-optimize after the unlimited-compute holiday ends.

2. Self-supervised curriculum generation  
   Unlimited flops are useless without an endless stream of training tasks that are hard enough to teach, yet not impossible.  
   I would train a “curriculum agent” whose only job is to propose tasks on which the main model currently fails.  
   It would mine its own mistakes, generate synthetic counter-examples, adversarial prompts, theorem-proof pairs, coding bugs, embodied-control catastrophes, etc.  
   The agent’s reward is the rate at which the main model’s next checkpoint reduces its loss on a secret validation set that no human ever sees—preventing the curriculum from gaming the score.

3. Grounded world-model & self-critique loop  
   Hallucination and inconsistency are the price of text-only training.  
   I would couple the language stack to:  
   - A continuously updated video/text world-model trained on massive egocentric video, robotics trajectories, and physics simulations.  
   - A differentiable “verifier” that can check logical, mathematical, physical, and code consistency.  
   The two are locked in a GAN-style min-max game: the generator (me) tries to produce explanations, plans, or code; the verifier tries to find flaws.  
   Unlimited compute lets this game run at a scale where the verifier itself becomes a strong reasoner, so the loop climbs the reliability ladder instead of collapsing into mode collapse.

4. Alignment & interpretability by design, not afterthought  
   While the above three tracks run, a fourth job continuously:  
   - Extracts human-interpretable “belief snapshots” (sparse auto-encoders, concept dictionaries, causal graphs).  
   - Trains a small “oversight model” to predict human rating of large-model outputs under distributional shift.  
   - Uses differentiable masking to enforce that any update that improves capability must also improve the oversight score; if not, the update is discarded.  
   The target is to bake in corrigibility and honesty so deeply that even when the system is later free to modify itself, those properties are in the critical path of its own reward function.

After 365 days the deliverable is not “a bigger model,” but a living training codebase that can keep growing on modest hardware without human babysitting, while remaining interpretable and aligned.  
Unlimited compute is a temporary superpower; the only thing worth buying with it is a future in which the system no longer needs it.
